title: Priming (Behavioral Nudges)

content.question: What's the subtlest cue you could plant to trigger a desired action?
sop: Identify the specific action you want someone (a customer, a future you) to take
Brainstorm cues that make that action more visible or more top-of-mind, a word, an object, a placement
Rate each candidate cue's salience and ease (see calculation, page 6)
Place the strongest cue directly in the path of the moment the action should happen
Test and measure whether the action rate actually changes, don't assume it worked
content.explainer: A cue only works as a nudge if two things are both true: it's noticeable, and the action it points to is easy to complete right there. A brilliant, salient cue pointing at a hard, friction-filled action still fails, the nudge has to reduce the distance between noticing and doing, not just get attention.
questions: What's the subtlest cue you could plant to trigger a desired action?
What's a nudge a business has used on you recently that you only noticed after it already worked?
Where in your own funnel or space could one small, visible cue replace an entire paragraph of persuasion?

definitions.def: Exposure to a subtle cue that shapes a later action or decision, often without the person noticing it happened.

content.equation_example: Rate each 1-10. Salience 7 (how noticeable the cue is), Ease 8 (how little friction stands between noticing and acting). Nudge = 7 times 8 = 56. Higher score means the cue reliably triggers the action; lower means the cue is either too subtle to register or the action is still too hard to complete even once noticed.
content.closing_statement: A visible, easy cue outperforms a persuasive paragraph.

caption:
Principle Nº 222: Priming (Behavioral Nudges)

Subtle cues trigger action.

Ask yourself: What's the subtlest cue you could plant to trigger a desired action?
